data.title: Covenant Logistics plans $40M–$50M net CapEx and anticipates fleet reduction amid improving freight market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Covenant Logistics Group, Inc. (CVLG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Parker provided an updated perspective on the freight market, stating, "We believe the freight market continues to evolve towards equilibrium between shippers and carriers. In fact, we might be at equilibrium now." He highlighted rising spot rates and improved revenue trends in January across all business units. Parker reported, "We have also secured a few low to mid-single-digit rate increases that take effect during the first quarter within our expedited fleet and anticipate additional increases across both Expedited and Dedicated to take effect early in the second quarter."
  • An equipment impairment charge was addressed, with CFO James Grant explaining that the company moved a group of assets to held-for-sale status and lowered expectations on disposition prices. The company plans a "modestly smaller fleet at the end of 2026 and only $40 million to $50 million of net CapEx for the year."
  • Grant also announced, "During the fourth quarter, we acquired the assets of a small truckload brokerage company. The business, which we will operate under the name, Star Logistics Solutions, has 2 niche customer bases...we expect Star to be accretive to earnings during the first half of 2026."
  • Parker emphasized a focus on margin improvement, stating, "Our goal is to reduce balance sheet leverage and improve return on capital."

Outlook

  • Management expressed optimism about improving freight fundamentals and operating leverage in 2026, but anticipated that improvements would likely come later in the year. The first quarter is expected to be affected by seasonality, extreme weather, and a developing market situation, with a potential margin squeeze in managed freight. The company plans to focus on enhancing margins through targeted rate increases and a continued exit from less profitable business.
  • Rate increases of low to mid-single digits in Expedited and Dedicated segments are expected to take effect in early 2026, with a "modestly smaller fleet" and net CapEx of $40 million to $50 million planned for the year.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated freight revenue for the quarter increased by 7.8% or approximately $19.5 million to $270.6 million. Adjusted operating income declined by 39.4% to $10.9 million, primarily due to margin compression in Expedited, Managed Freight, and Warehousing segments, partially offset by improvement in Dedicated.
  • Net indebtedness as of December 31 rose by $76.9 million to $296.6 million, with an adjusted leverage ratio of approximately 2.3x and debt-to-capital ratio of 42.3%.
  • The Expedited segment reported an adjusted operating ratio of 97.2% for the quarter, which management described as below expectations, partly impacted by the U.S. government shutdown. Dedicated's adjusted operating ratio of 92.2% was the best for any quarter during the year, with the fleet growing by 90 average tractors or about 6.3% compared to the prior year.
  • Managed Freight revenue increased with the Star acquisition but faced margin compression. Warehousing revenue rose by $1.1 million, though adjusted operating income declined by $1.6 million due to start-up costs and inefficiencies.

Q&A

  • Jason Seidl, TD Cowen, Research Division: Asked if low to mid-single-digit price increases in Expedited were the average now or the start of a trend and about expectations for the bid cycle. Parker replied, "It's both. The average is kind of around that 3.5% number for the first 3 weeks of January...it's something that's continuing to build some momentum."
  • Seidl questioned warehousing revenue/profit trends. President M. Bunn replied, "Q1 will be better than Q4 and Q2 of this year will be better than Q1. So it will incrementally get better every quarter."
  • Seidl asked about leverage and comfort with the current ratio. Grant responded, "Our leverage today after this acquisition is a little bit above where we would kind of want it longer term...I think we'll start to see it improve."
  • Jeffrey Kauffman, Vertical Research Partners, LLC: Inquired about equipment adjustments and potential gains/losses on sale. Grant explained, "I don't think it's going to be a large loss or a large gain."
  • Kauffman probed fleet count trends post-adjustments. Bunn said Expedited count would "trend down slightly, maybe 25 trucks a quarter-ish," while Dedicated would stay "flattish" with margin improvements.
  • Reed Seay, Stephens Inc.: Asked about Managed Freight revenue outlook. Grant expects it to be "somewhere below where we landed in Q4...for Q1 of 2026," with incremental improvement after that.
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research, LLC: Sought an update on market conviction. Parker stated, "I'm absolutely much more excited right now than I was 3 months ago...I just think -- I just really believe guys that we are on the beginning stages of the trucking industry getting back to where it needs to be at."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on pricing trends, fleet strategy, and profitability, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone, probing for details on execution and margin recovery.
  • Management's tone was optimistic and confident, particularly on market recovery and rate increases, as evidenced by Parker's statement, "I'm absolutely much more excited right now than I was 3 months ago."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence has increased, shifting from cautious optimism to strong enthusiasm about future market conditions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management's language has shifted from preparing for a challenging environment in Q3 to expressing heightened excitement about market recovery and operating leverage opportunities in Q4.
  • Strategic focus has moved from resilience and cost control to growth through targeted rate increases, capital efficiency, and accretive acquisitions.
  • Guidance now emphasizes a "modestly smaller fleet," lower CapEx, and improved margin targets, compared to the previous quarter's broader emphasis on cost management and fleet maintenance.
  • Analysts' questions have evolved from concerns about regulatory impacts and cost control to inquiries about growth execution, integration of acquisitions, and the timeline for margin improvement.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing risks from regulatory changes, cost inflation, insurance and claims risk, a potential U.S. government shutdown, and severe weather, which could affect first-quarter performance.
  • Analysts raised questions about leverage, equipment value realization, and the pace at which operating margins can recover.
  • Management's mitigation strategies include reducing fleet size, focusing on higher-yield business, and maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation and integration of acquisitions.

Final Takeaway

Covenant Logistics Group, Inc. enters 2026 with a focus on disciplined fleet management, targeted rate increases, and operational efficiency, supported by a recent acquisition and a capital plan that limits net CapEx to $40 million to $50 million. Management’s optimism about an improving freight market, combined with strategic margin enhancement and leverage reduction initiatives, signals a clear execution-driven agenda for the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Worst performing industrials stocks in the past month
data.description:
As the first month of 2026 comes to an end this week and earnings season accelerates, below is a list of the 10 worst-performing industrials stocks with market capitalizations of $10B or more in the past one month.

The list includes stocks from various industrial sub-sectors such as Passenger Airlines, Aerospace and Defense, Data Processing & Outsourced Services, Research and Consulting Services, and Human Resource & Employment Services, ranked based on one-month price performance.

The list is topped by Joby Aviation (JOBY), with a one-month decline of 17.73%. Broadridge Financial Solutions (BR) and Axon Enterprise (AXON) follow closely behind, with UL Solutions (ULS) and Paychex (PAYX) rounding out the bottom five performers.

While Axon Enterprise (AXON) carries a Strong Sell Quant Rating of 1.32, others on the list maintain more favorable ratings despite their recent price dips: United Airlines Holdings (UAL) holds a Buy rating of 4.18 and Symbotic (SYM) has a Buy rating of 3.82. The majority of the list, including Equifax (EFX) and TransUnion (TRU), currently hold a Hold rating.

Here is the list:

  1. Joby Aviation (JOBY), 1 month performance percentage: -17.73%

  2. Broadridge Financial Solutions (BR), 1 month performance percentage: -13.82%

  3. Axon Enterprise (AXON), 1 month performance percentage: -12.22%

  4. UL Solutions (ULS), 1 month performance percentage: -11.95%

  5. Paychex (PAYX), 1 month performance percentage: -10.32%

  6. Equifax (EFX), 1 month performance percentage: -8.16%

  7. TransUnion (TRU), 1 month performance percentage: -7.82%

  8. SS&C Technologies (SSNC), 1 month performance percentage: -7.27%

  9. United Airlines Holdings (UAL), 1 month performance percentage: -6.74%

  10. Symbotic (SYM), 1 month performance percentage: -6.05%

Industrials ETFs: (XLI), (VIS), (FXR), (IYJ), (FIDU), and (RSPN)

data.title: GE Vernova raised to Buy at Guggenheim as market underestimates cash generation
data.description:
GE Vernova (GEV) +0.8% in Friday's trading as Guggenheim upgraded shares to Buy from Neutral with a $910 price target, believing the market still may be underestimating the company's potential for cash generation and capital returns.

Guggenheim analyst Joseph Osha said he likes the potential for further upside relative to expectations at the free cash flow level and at the EBITDA margin level for the company's electrification segment.

Wall Street has tended to underestimate GE Vernova's (GEV) free cash flow, Osha said, noting the updated 2025-28 FCF target released by the company was $24B-plus, up from $22B previously, adding that GEV has substantially more ability to return capital than investors may appreciate, and the company could literally double buybacks between now and 2028 without drawing down cash.

Osha also believes GE Vernova's (GEV) electrification segment revenues can reach ~$20B in 2028 without any capital additions beyond what is currently planned and has the potential to exit 2028 at an electrification EBITDA margin of ~25%.

data.title: Regeneron outlines 18 new Phase III studies and expects four FDA approvals in 2026 while advancing EYLEA HD and DUPIXENT growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (REGN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Leonard Schleifer reported "fourth quarter total revenue up 3% year-over-year, driven by double-digit net sales growth for 3 of our leading products." Schleifer highlighted global net product sales for DUPIXENT increasing by 32% and LIBTAYO by 13% at constant exchange rates, while "EYLEA HD in the United States grew by 66%." He stated, "DUPIXENT is currently the most widely used innovative branded antibody medicine with more than 1.4 million patients on therapy globally" and is "well positioned for future growth." Schleifer underscored LIBTAYO's progress in adjuvant CSCC and noted it "continues to build share in advanced non-small cell lung cancer, where in the U.S., it is now the second most prescribed immunotherapy in the first-line setting."

  • Schleifer announced, "We anticipate at least 4 FDA approvals, including 3 for new molecular entities across 3 distinct modalities plus approval for the EYLEA HD prefilled syringe as well as several additional regulatory submissions." He described plans to initiate "18 additional Phase III studies with cumulative target enrollment of approximately 35,000 patients over multiple years, setting the foundation for Regeneron's next wave of potential blockbuster products."

  • Chief Scientific Officer George Yancopoulos discussed clinical momentum: "In 2026, we plan to build on our established leadership in ophthalmology as well as immunology and inflammation while advancing key late-stage programs." He described data expected from "our Phase III study in geographic atrophy in the second half of 2026" and plans to "initiate clinical development for a long-acting antibody targeting a novel genetically validated target for glaucoma."

  • Executive Vice President of Commercial Marion McCourt highlighted "EYLEA HD net sales reached $506 million, representing 18% sequential growth" and noted "new real-world market data shows that on average, patients with ongoing anti-VEGF therapy who switched to EYLEA HD were able to extend their treatment duration by almost 4 weeks."

  • CFO Christopher Fenimore stated, "Fourth quarter 2025 total revenues of $3.9 billion grew 3% compared to the prior year," and "fourth quarter diluted net income per share was $11.44 on net income of $1.2 billion." He added, "Regeneron generated $4.1 billion in free cash flow in 2025 and ended the quarter with cash and marketable securities less debt of $16.2 billion."

Outlook

  • Fenimore reported, "We expect 2026 R&D spend to be in the range of $5.9 billion to $6.1 billion." He guided for "2026 SG&A to be in the range of $2.5 billion to $2.65 billion," and "gross margin on net product sales to be in the range of 83% to 84%." Capital expenditures for 2026 are expected to be "in the range of $1.1 billion to $1.3 billion."

  • Schleifer stated, "We anticipate at least 4 FDA approvals" and expect "registration-enabling data from multiple programs." The company plans to "continue prudently deploying capital through share repurchases, dividends and complementary business development."

Financial Results

  • Fenimore attributed revenue growth to "higher collaboration revenue driven by strong global DUPIXENT sales growth, continued growth in net sales of EYLEA HD and LIBTAYO as well as higher other revenue, partially offset by lower net sales of EYLEA 2 milligrams."

  • Regeneron's share of profits from the Sanofi collaboration "grew 42% versus the prior year, primarily driven by DUPIXENT and improving collaboration margins."

  • Other revenue "grew 33% in the fourth quarter to $239 million," including $179 million from royalty income. The company "returned $3.8 billion to shareholders in 2025, primarily through $3.4 billion in share repurchases."

Q&A

  • Alexandria Hammond, Wolfe Research, LLC: Asked about the timing for LIBTAYO plus fianlimab readouts. Ryan Crowe responded, "First half is the best estimate at this time. In terms of the adjuvant timing, that's also in the first half. They may coincide, they may not. Once we have the data, we will read it out shortly thereafter."

  • Christopher Raymond, Raymond James: Asked about DUPIXENT IP runway. Schleifer responded, "No, no additional comments. I thought Sanofi did a good job laying out what the realm of possibilities are." Yancopoulos emphasized follow-on opportunities in the space.

  • Salveen Richter, Goldman Sachs: Asked about metastatic melanoma data. Yancopoulos explained the study is "largely powered to get an effect in terms of the primary endpoint, which is the PFS analogous to the current combination standard of care."

  • Tyler Van Buren, TD Cowen: Asked about R&D strategy. Yancopoulos said, "We make our choices based on the most powerful available data and technology that can guide decision-making, which is large-scale human genetics."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with focused questions on clinical data timing, IP strategy, and R&D priorities. There was limited skepticism and more interest in pipeline progress and product differentiation.

  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing growth and innovation: "I have never been more excited about the breadth, depth and potential impact of our pipeline." During Q&A, responses were measured and occasionally deflective when discussing competitive or strategic details.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a confident stance, while analysts' tone shifted from operational concerns to greater interest in pipeline catalysts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance specificity increased, with explicit ranges for R&D, SG&A, gross margin, and capital expenditures for 2026, versus prior general commentary about a "mid-teens percentage increase" in R&D.

  • The pipeline focus expanded further, with 18 new Phase III studies planned versus several registrational programs discussed previously.

  • Product growth rates for DUPIXENT and LIBTAYO remained strong, but EYLEA HD's growth accelerated with sequential demand and new label enhancements.

  • Analysts’ questions this quarter focused more on key pipeline milestones and differentiation versus operational execution and regulatory setbacks last quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Schleifer noted ongoing U.S. government negotiations on drug pricing: "We are actively engaging in constructive discussions with the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services and other federal agencies."

  • Management cited continued competition for EYLEA 2 milligrams, with pressure expected to intensify in the second half of 2026 as "multiple biosimilar products are launched in the United States."

  • Patient affordability concerns persist, with Regeneron extending its matching program up to $200 million.

  • Regulatory and manufacturing risks remain for EYLEA HD, with FDA review of the prefilled syringe application and backup plans in place.

Final Takeaway

Regeneron's management conveyed a strong outlook for 2026, emphasizing commercial momentum for DUPIXENT, LIBTAYO, and EYLEA HD, as well as a robust pipeline with at least four FDA approvals anticipated and 18 new Phase III studies planned. While competitive and regulatory headwinds persist, the company is focused on maximizing portfolio growth, expanding in new therapeutic areas, and sustaining capital returns to shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tech Voices: DeepSeek moving to get Nvidia chips, Anthropic-Pentagon
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) has been given the green light by Chinese authorities to buy Nvidia (NVDA) H200 AI chips, subject to certain conditions that are still being worked out, according to Reuters, which cited people close to the matter.

Reuters said Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang told reporters in Taiwan on Thursday that he hadn't heard DeepSeek had been approved to buy the chips. Nvidia did not return a request by Reuters for comment on the DeepSeek approval.  

Earlier this week, Reuters reported that ByteDance (BDNCE), Alibaba (BABA), and Tencent (TCEHY) had been granted conditional approval to buy more than 400,000 of the chips.  

  • Anthropic (ANTHRO) and the Pentagon are reportedly still debating what safeguards should be put in place to prevent its technology from being used for domestic surveillance or targeting weapons without sufficient human oversight. 

According to Reuters, talks between Anthropic and the Defense Department are now at a standstill over the matter. The DOD awarded a $200M contract to Anthropic in July. 

Other AI companies that have been awarded DOD contracts include Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google, xAI (X.AI), and OpenAI (OPENAI), Reuters noted.

data.title: Aon outlines mid-single-digit organic revenue growth for 2026 while accelerating data center and middle market strategies
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Aon plc (AON) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Gregory Case, President and CEO, stated that "2025 was a year of great strategic progress and performance milestones for Aon," highlighting the disciplined execution of the 3x3 plan and the acceleration of the Aon United strategy. Case emphasized advancements in integrating risk and human capital, expanding client leadership, and leveraging the Aon Business Services (ABS) platform to drive innovation and efficiency.
  • Case announced the expansion of the Data Center Lifecycle Insurance Protection Program (DCLP), increasing total capacity to $2.5 billion, and highlighted new product launches such as Aon Broker Copilot and Claims Copilot. He described a “$1 billion expansion” of the DCLP and the launch of the first-ever data center-specific treaty aligning up to $5 billion of capital.
  • The integration of NFP was described as making great progress, with Case noting strong producer retention and synergy as NFP is onboarded onto the ABS platform. The company also executed the sale of NFP Wealth, enhancing capital flexibility.
  • Case reported "6% organic revenue growth for the second straight year, 90 basis points of adjusted operating margin expansion, strong adjusted EPS growth and double-digit free cash flow growth."
  • Edmund Reese, Executive VP & Chief Financial Officer, stated: "Our full year performance is right in line with our guidance for mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth, adjusted operating margin expansion, strong earnings and double-digit free cash flow growth." Reese highlighted a total capital position of $7 billion for 2026, supported by strong cash generation and disciplined portfolio management.

Outlook

  • The company guided for 2026 mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth, 70 to 80 basis points of adjusted operating margin expansion, strong adjusted EPS growth, and double-digit free cash flow growth. Reese explained, "We expect mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth, fueled by recurring new business wins... and accretive growth in the middle market, including revenue synergies from NFP."
  • Reese outlined expectations for $4.3 billion of free cash flow generation from operating income and working capital improvements, with a $300 million tax impact from the NFP Wealth sale.
  • Management reiterated commitment to at least $1 billion in share repurchases for 2026, while evaluating inorganic growth opportunities in high-margin, high-growth segments.

Financial Results

  • For full year 2025, organic revenue growth was 6% and total revenue increased 9% year-over-year to $17 billion. Adjusted operating margin reached 32.4%, up 90 basis points, and adjusted EPS was $17.07, representing 9% year-over-year growth. Free cash flow increased 14% to $3.2 billion.
  • In Q4, organic revenue growth was 5% and total revenue increased 4% year-over-year to $4.3 billion. Adjusted operating margin expanded by 220 basis points to 35.5%. Adjusted EPS was $4.85, up 10%. Free cash flow for the quarter reached $1.3 billion, increasing 16%.
  • Commercial Risk and Reinsurance segments delivered 6% or better organic growth in Q4; Construction continued double-digit growth. Reinsurance delivered 8% growth, with strong contributions from insurance-linked securities and the Strategy and Technology Group.
  • Health Solutions grew 2% in Q4, while Wealth generated 2% growth, in line with previous guidance.
  • Operating income increased 11% in Q4 to $1.5 billion. Restructuring savings totaled $160 million for the year, with $50 million in Q4. Interest expense for Q4 was $191 million, and the tax rate for the quarter was 20%.

Q&A

  • Jian Huang, Morgan Stanley, asked about competition for talent and retention in key growth areas. Case responded, "We are uniquely positioned to bring talent into the firm ... we're well up on a net basis from a talent standpoint."
  • Huang inquired about acquisition opportunities given market volatility. Reese explained the balanced capital allocation model, stating, "We are in a position of strength with $7 billion in available capital ... we'll continue to scale in middle market through NFP, particularly in North America Commercial Risk."
  • Elyse Greenspan, Wells Fargo, asked about timing and deployment of $7 billion in capital and reasons for setting $1 billion for share buybacks. Reese clarified there is no specific deal targeted for early 2026 and the company aims for strategic flexibility.
  • Greenspan also asked about data center contributions to organic growth. Case noted the opportunity is "monumental" and "just beginning," while Reese highlighted double-digit growth in construction as evidence.
  • Matthew Heimermann, Citi, questioned whether market share in data centers correlates with historical share. Case responded, "Past is not prologue. This is net new innovation ... our obligation on behalf of clients ... is massively more innovation."
  • Charles Lederer, BMO, asked about AAU program savings and pacing. Reese confirmed AAU program completion in 2026 with savings increased from $350 million to $450 million. Lederer also inquired about free cash flow guidance; Reese explained the $4.3 billion figure is prior to the tax impact from the NFP Wealth sale and is driven by operating cash flows and working capital improvements.
  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI, asked about capital deployment and acquired EBITDA targets. Reese indicated capital would be deployed based on pipeline opportunities and reiterated a desire for high capital deployment in NFP with balance across risk and human capital segments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on capital deployment, talent retention, M&A opportunities, and the scalability of new business areas, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions were direct and probed for specifics on data center strategy and use of available capital.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone during both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently using phrases like "well positioned," "unique opportunity," and "excited about this opportunity." Case and Reese both emphasized strategic momentum and capital strength.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, with slightly increased emphasis on innovation and new business areas. Analysts continued to press on capital allocation and market positioning but showed heightened interest in the scalability of the data center and middle market strategies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language for 2026 remains focused on mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth and further margin expansion, consistent with the previous quarter's outlook but now supported by full-year results and a larger capital base.
  • Strategic focus has intensified on data center opportunities and accelerating NFP integration, while the previous quarter placed more emphasis on the early stages of these initiatives.
  • Key metrics such as revenue growth, margin expansion, and free cash flow growth remained robust, with incremental improvements in margin and operating leverage highlighted this quarter.
  • Both analyst and management sentiment remained confident; however, the current quarter saw more probing on innovation scalability and the use of capital for inorganic growth.
  • The management team signaled greater conviction in the compounding benefits of new business wins, revenue-generating hires, and ABS-driven efficiencies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged competitive intensity in the talent market, stating they are "not immune" but continue to invest in priority growth areas.
  • Reinsurance faces headwinds from "softer Jan 1 property renewals with rate declines of 15% to 20%," yet management expects to offset this with higher limits and growing demand for analytics.
  • Health Solutions experienced delayed closed sales and slower discretionary spend in talent consulting, with management expressing confidence in a rebound as conditions normalize.
  • Analysts raised concerns about capital deployment timing and the scalability of new product initiatives, particularly in data centers and the middle market.

Final Takeaway

Aon enters 2026 with strong momentum, highlighting the successful execution of its 3x3 plan and the durability of its business model. The company is accelerating its data center and middle market strategies, supported by a robust capital position and ongoing investments in talent and technology. Management remains confident in achieving mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth, continued margin expansion, and double-digit free cash flow growth, underpinned by disciplined capital allocation and innovation across its solution lines.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: What's next for UnitedHealth after this week's selloff?
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (UNH) faced a punishing week as shares tumbled nearly 20% on Tuesday following a one-two punch of disappointing Q4 earnings and an unexpected Medicare Advantage rate proposal from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. The healthcare giant’s revenue guidance for 2026 fell well below Street expectations, signaling what would be its first annual revenue decline in more than three decades.

Still, despite the sharp selloff, analyst sentiment remains mixed, with several experts maintaining Buy ratings even while they acknowledged the challenging road ahead.

What Happened to UnitedHealth?

UnitedHealth reported Q4 2025 adjusted earnings per share of $2.11, in line with consensus. Meanwhile, the firm’s revenue of $113.2 billion missed estimates by approximately $520 million despite 12% year-over-year growth. The company also took a $1.6 billion post-tax charge related to restructuring, divestitures, and lingering cyberattack costs.

Investors were more concerned with the 2026 outlook. Management guided for revenue of more than $439 billion, significantly below the $456 billion analyst consensus, and adjusted EPS of greater than $17.75.

The earnings report came just hours after the CMS proposed a net average payment increase of just 0.09% for Medicare Advantage plans in 2027. This was dramatically below the 4% to 6% increase analysts had anticipated.

This proposal sent managed care stocks tumbling in after-hours trading, with Humana (HUM), CVS Health (CVS), and Elevance Health (ELV) all experiencing significant declines. The combination of weak guidance and regulatory headwinds wiped out more than $50 billion in market capitalization for UNH in a single trading session.

Management indicated the company would prioritize margin recovery over membership growth in 2026, expecting Medicare Advantage membership to contract by 1.3 to 1.4 million members. The medical care ratio came in at 89.1% for 2025, up from 85.5% in 2024, reflecting pressure from Medicare funding reductions and elevated medical cost trends. CEO Stephen Hemsley noted that the company had “confronted challenges directly” and emerged from 2025 as “a much stronger company.”

What Do Analysts Say About UnitedHealth’s Future?

Despite the sharp decline, several analysts maintained bullish stances on UnitedHealth, viewing the selloff as overdone. Bulls pointed to the company’s strong cash flow generation of $19.7 billion in 2025, its strategic shift toward margin recovery, and the potential for CMS to revise the final Medicare Advantage rate higher by April (as has occurred in eight of the past ten years). The company’s investments in AI and automation, expected to drive $1 billion in cost savings in 2026, also provide reasons for optimism.

Bears, however, highlighted the structural challenges facing the Medicare Advantage business, with rising medical costs and flat government reimbursements creating a widening margin squeeze. The medical care ratio remains elevated at nearly 89%, well above historical levels of 82% to 85%, the bears pointed out.

Some analysts also expressed concern about the company’s ability to meet even its conservative guidance, given the uncertainty around 2027 rates and the potential for further membership losses.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

Mike Zaccardi, CFA, CMT, Rating: Buy: “The stock appears attractive from a valuation perspective, but the technicals clearly show the bears being in control.”- UnitedHealth: The Bearish 1-2 Punch Pressuring Shares, And Why I’m Bullish

Danil Sereda, Rating: Buy: “Despite the clear risks of a further correction, I believe the stock price has already absorbed most of the negatives. The forward EPS and revenue growth rates aren’t going to fade, in my view, as long as the updated management team keeps executing on its plans and guidance… I see upside potential of more than 22% from the current stock price.” - UnitedHealth Q4 Earnings Review: The Selloff Looks Overdone After Results

Cyn Research, Rating: Buy: “Times of uncertainty are often the best buying opportunities, as in other scenarios involving more positive sentiment, a lot of future growth is already priced in, increasing the potential downside… The turnaround might take longer than expected but is still one worth betting on.” - UnitedHealth Group: Recovery Might Take Longer, But Contrarians Know What To Do

Jonathan Weber, Rating: Buy: “It is pretty clear that UnitedHealth has problems: there are political headwinds when it comes to Medicare funding, there are steep cost increases, etc. But to me, it is also clear that UNH has opportunities… For risk-averse investors, UNH isn’t a good choice. However, for enterprising investors with a longer-term investment horizon, UnitedHealth is appealing at the current price.” - UnitedHealth: Outlook Makes Shares Plunge

Quad 7 Capital, Rating: Buy: “This was quarter was less than what we expected out of UNH, and the earnings inflection confirmation that we got was weaker than expected. Yes, things are getting better, but not as much improvement as we expected… Long-term, we still see upside. You figure $17.75 in EPS puts shares at a below-market multiple now of 16X.” - UnitedHealth Group: Double Whammy Disaster

The Techie, Rating: Hold: “This pullback should not automatically be viewed as a buying opportunity, as weakness is broad-based across the managed care sector… UNH’s rising medical care ratio is a real fundamental concern. While Optum remains a bright spot and continues to be the strategic asset that differentiates UNH from peers, its strength is no longer enough to fully offset pressure in the insurance business.” - UnitedHealth Group Stock Is Not Attractive Yet: Caution Is Warranted

Julia Ostian, Rating: Buy: “Losing such a massive amount of market cap for a powerhouse like UnitedHealth looks like a major, major overreaction to me… Just like the dip we saw in August, I expect a rebound once the market realizes that UnitedHealth is fine, so I even consider adding more on this drop.” - UnitedHealth Group: I Might Add On This Massive Overreaction

Edmund Ingham, Rating: Hold: “At this point—and I would note it remains early days and the shock factor may have driven the stock down further than is truly warranted—I don’t anticipate a fast recovery for the share price of Humana or UnitedHealth… The only sensible decision an investor can make today is to hold onto stock and monitor developments.” - UnitedHealth, Humana: CMS’ Rate Decision Has Crushed Them, And There’s No Quick Fix

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

UnitedHealth currently carries a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system. The company continues to demonstrate strong profitability, earning an A+ grade, with a net income margin of 4% that significantly exceeds the sector median of 0.5%. Valuation also scores well at B+, helped by the a sell-off in the stock that now sees it down almost 50% over the past year.

However, growth metrics present significant concerns, receiving an F grade. Forward EBITDA growth stands at -8% compared to the sector’s 8%, while forward EPS growth of -11% trails the sector average of 13%. Momentum also remains weak at D+, with one-year price performance of -34% underperforming the sector’s -4%. Earnings revisions present a mixed picture at B-, with 19 upward EPS revisions offset by 6 downward revisions.

What Is UnitedHealth?

The Basics: UnitedHealth Group Incorporated, founded in 1974 and headquartered in Eden Prairie, Minnesota, is a diversified healthcare and well-being company operating globally. The company is organized into two complementary businesses: UnitedHealthcare, which offers health benefit plans and services for individuals, employers, and government programs, including Medicare and Medicaid; and Optum, an information and technology-enabled health services business operating through Optum Health, Optum Insight, and Optum Rx segments.

Competitive Landscape: UnitedHealth faces competition from several major players in the healthcare and insurance industry, including Humana Inc. and Elevance Health Inc. The company holds the largest position in the Medicare Advantage market with approximately 29% of all enrollees, serving roughly 9.9 million members. However, the industry faces significant regulatory challenges, with CMS reimbursement rates emerging as a key pressure point and rising medical cost trends threatening profitability across the managed care sector.

data.title: Olin outlines $100M–$120M cost savings for 2026 as turnaround and pricing actions intensify
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Olin Corporation (OLN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kenneth Lane, President and CEO, reported that the fourth quarter results were "significantly below our expectations" due to operational issues at the Freeport, Texas chlorinated organics asset and third-party raw material constraints, as well as a "sharp decline in chlorine pipeline demand in an already seasonally weaker quarter."
  • Lane highlighted the announcement of a long-term EDC supply agreement with Braskem and the expansion of infrastructure in Brazil to grow caustic sales in 2026. He also noted significant growth in European Epoxy business contracts starting in Q1 2026, positioning Olin as the last integrated supplier of Epoxy in Europe.
  • The Winchester business responded to lower ammunition demand by accelerating inventory reductions and rightsizing its cost structure.
  • Lane stated, "Cash generation is a high priority for Olin, especially in the trough environment that we're in," with $321 million of operating cash flow generated and net debt held flat versus year-end 2024.
  • Lane detailed the Beyond250 structural cost reduction program, saying, "In 2025, we delivered $44 million in structural cost savings and we expect to add an incremental $100 million to $120 million of annual Beyond250 savings during 2026."
  • Todd Slater, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "Despite the challenges we encountered that impacted our adjusted EBITDA during the fourth quarter and throughout 2025, I'm pleased to report that we successfully achieved our 2025 cash flow and working capital objectives."

Outlook

  • Lane projected "first quarter earnings lower than fourth quarter 2025" due to "continued seasonally weaker demand and higher costs in our CAPV business."
  • Winchester results are expected to "modestly improve from the fourth quarter, with higher commercial ammunition volume and pricing to offset rising copper and brass costs as well as lower operating costs from our new operating model."
  • Management expects "full year 2026 chlor-alkali outlook remains challenging" and continues to anticipate pressure on global vinyls pricing with headwinds from rising U.S. natural gas, power, and feedstock costs.

Financial Results

  • $321 million in operating cash flow was generated in Q4, enabling net debt to be held flat compared to year-end 2024.
  • Working capital reductions contributed $248 million in cash for the year, excluding tax payment timing.
  • Available liquidity at year-end was reported at $1 billion.
  • Capital spending for 2026 is targeted at approximately $200 million, with a focus on sustaining capital expenditures.
  • Slater noted, "We anticipate receiving refunds from prior years related to the clean hydrogen production tax credits... we expect 2026 to essentially be a cash-free tax year, plus or minus $20 million."

Q&A

  • Aleksey Yefremov, KeyBanc: Asked about the sharp decline in chlorine pipeline demand and its persistence into Q1. Lane answered, "That really happened in December, late in December... I don't expect to see what we saw at the end of December happened again in the first quarter, but we're not going to see a large bounce back in demand until we get into the warmer weather months."
  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about competitor capacity closures. Lane responded, "We have been seeing rationalization of capacity occurring over the last 12 to 18 months... when demand does recover... we're ready when it does."
  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research: Asked about military demand trends at Winchester. Lane explained, "Significant growth in revenue related to military, both domestic and international... we are seeing weekly improvements in out-the-door sales at retailers."
  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi: Asked about downstream participation in chlorovinyls. Lane indicated ongoing evaluation of expansion options but no definitive decisions yet.
  • Hassan Ahmed, Alembic: Sought clarity on Q1 guidance impacts from energy and weather events. Lane provided a year-over-year bridge, highlighting a "significant increase in turnaround spend year-over-year" and higher power and natural gas costs.
  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium: Asked about the $70 million stranded costs from Dow's PO-related closure. Lane stated, "That is a very clear focus for us to be able to do that as we wind those assets down, but that is going to be something that happens over time."
  • Joshua Spector, UBS: Questioned the impact of downtime and outages on Q4 and Q1. Lane cited very low caustic inventories leading to volume constraints in Q1.
  • Other analysts probed on energy hedging, Braskem EDC benefits, Winchester margin improvement, and cost management, with management often refraining from quantifying specifics but reiterating focus on cost actions and pricing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative, focusing on volume constraints, cost headwinds, and the sustainability of margin improvements, pressing for clarity on inventory, guidance, and the impact of one-time items.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly defensive tone, frequently emphasizing cost discipline and operational control. Lane reiterated, "We're going to be really focused on everything that we can do to ensure that we're becoming a more efficient company, reducing our costs in the face of a very difficult market."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts appeared more cautious, with increased emphasis on navigating headwinds and managing costs rather than expecting near-term market recovery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from anticipating stable ECU values and incremental improvement to highlighting lower first quarter earnings and persistent market pressures.
  • Strategic focus has moved to an intensified cost-cutting effort (Beyond250) and operational discipline amid continued demand weakness.
  • Analysts in both quarters probed for details on cost management and recovery timing, but in Q4 the questioning grew more pointed on volume shortfalls and the challenge of offsetting stranded costs.
  • Management confidence in cost actions remains, but optimism about market recovery has moderated compared to the prior quarter.
  • Key metric changes include a focus on operating cash flow ($321 million in Q4) and a new $100 million–$120 million cost savings target for 2026, whereas Q3 focused on expectations for structural savings and the impact of the clean hydrogen tax credit.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing headwinds: seasonally weak demand, higher turnaround and energy costs, stranded costs from Dow's closure, and persistent pricing pressure in global vinyls.
  • Risks include volume constraints from very low caustic inventories, uncertainty in demand recovery timing, and continued competition from subsidized Asian producers.
  • Mitigation strategies center on cost reduction (Beyond250), operational discipline, and selective price increases in Winchester and caustic soda.
  • Analysts remain concerned about the sustainability of margin improvements, cost pass-through ability, and the timing and magnitude of market recovery.

Final Takeaway

Olin management acknowledges a challenging environment marked by operational disruptions, cost headwinds, and weak demand, but underscores its commitment to disciplined operations, aggressive cost-cutting, and cash generation. The company expects lower earnings in early 2026 but is targeting $100 million to $120 million in structural cost savings during the year, expanding partnerships in Brazil, and pursuing margin improvements through pricing and efficiency actions, positioning itself to capitalize when market conditions improve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amgen ends collaboration agreement with Kyowa Kirin for eczema asset
data.description:
• Amgen (AMGN) has terminated a licensing and collaboration agreement signed in 2021 with Japanese pharma Kyowa Kirin (KYKOF) regarding the development of the biologic roctinlimab for atopic dermatitis.
  • In 2021, Amgen paid $400M for the rights to co-develop and commercialize the asset outside Japan. Kyowa Kirin was also eligible for up to $850M in milestone payments.
  • Kyowa Kirin now regains full rights to roctinlimab. The company noted that Amgen ended the agreement due to a strategic portfolio prioritization.
  • The companies released phase 3 long-term data on roctinlimab in September 2025. 
  • Despite the setback, Kyowa Kirin said it would file an application for the candidate with the FDA in the first half of the year.

data.title: Worst performing materials stocks in the past month
data.description:
As the first month of 2026 comes to an end and earnings season accelerates, below is a list of the 10 worst performing materials stocks with market capitalizations ranging from approximately $11B to $57B in the past one month. 

The list is topped by International Paper Company (IP), with a Quant Rating of 2.72 (Hold) and the only negative performance on the list at -2.16%. Crown Holdings (CCK) and Avery Dennison (AVY) follow, with RPM International (RPM) and Martin Marietta Materials (MLM) rounding out the rest of the top five.

The remaining stocks on the list show modest gains but still represent the weakest performers in the materials sector. Notably, RPM International (RPM) carries a Sell rating with a Quant Rating of 1.95, while Steel Dynamics (STLD) stands out with a Strong Buy rating of 4.58 despite appearing on this list of underperformers.

Here is the list:

  1. International Paper Company (IP), 1 month performance percentage: -2.16%

  2. Crown Holdings (CCK), 1 month performance percentage: +0.56%

  3. Avery Dennison (AVY), 1 month performance percentage: +1.49%

  4. RPM International (RPM), 1 month performance percentage: +2.06%

  5. Martin Marietta Materials (MLM), 1 month performance percentage: +2.52%

  6. Vulcan Materials (VMC), 1 month performance percentage: +2.55%

  7. International Flavors & Fragrances (IFF), 1 month performance percentage: +2.72%

  8. Air Products and Chemicals (APD), 1 month performance percentage: +3.30%

  9. Steel Dynamics (STLD), 1 month performance percentage: +5.26%

  10. Packaging Corporation of America (PKG), 1 month performance percentage: +5.48%
